item.title: Issue with the second Addendum
item.content:encoded:
I think it's a pretty creepy SCP object. so it get's my upvote.

one thing that irks me is the second Addendum.
it's states that using 1109 on D-Class, which may then come into contact with other SCP objects, is pending approval.
and it sounds like this Unnamed Doctor, who is stated to have Level 2 clearance earlier in the article, is the making the call if it will be approved.

maybe it's just because of the way it's written but someone with level 2 clearance should be the one to decide if the risk of cross contamination is worth it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can just imagine some idiot at Aceso going, "Long-term effects? What long-term effects? We know it works, earnings are down this quarter, just sell it already!"
+1

item.title: Image Usage
item.content:encoded: SCP-1109's image was removed because we could not properly determine whether or not the image was properly licensed or attributed. It may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact me, Dr Poke, or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't feel nothing at all~!

…Sorry, that was all I could think of. Great scip, though! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually loved the incident log, specifically because it is an anecdote, and really explains the insidious nature of the SCP. But what really gets my upvote is the mention of one of the anomalous items being found in civilian possession. The thought of whether it broke containment or wasn't fully contained to begin with has always given me the chills, so bravo. The rest of the article was a bit lackluster, but ehh… Good tone, and great first try. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I imagine some people who are exposed to this item are able to cope with it. The item doesn't just cut out your ability to feel pain, it completely removes any ability to feel physical sensations. As you might imagine, the majority don't take this very well. I'll edit in something saying that it doesn't happen to everyone, as reading it through again it didn't come across that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually meant the wording of the sentence didn't make sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
^ Seconded.

I'm also not sold on all of the secondary characteristics of this thing, the masochism and all that. There are real people who don't feel pain (and, bizarrely, there are real people who weren't born not feeling pain, but no longer do), and I'm fairly positive not all of them are into slicing themselves up to try and feel something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one's really good, and the addenda help drive home just what being affected by this thing does to a person.

I do question this sentence:

However, as the condition progresses even the violent or severe physical sensations become completely numb.

It just doesn't make any sense. It also seems somewhat redundant given what's around it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This thing doesn't need a hook; any piercing or cutting implement will do nicely. [/rimshot]

Seriously though, this is actually quite well done, ESPECIALLY as a first-time job. There are a few word-usage and grammar nitpicks scattered around (affect/effect is a personal pet peeve) but nowhere near bad enough to downvote. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hoped someone would pick up on that! That was the sense I was trying to get across with the specifics of the effect and the stamp for Aceso Medical- something that could have been wonderful if they'd been able to work out the kinks, but is instead useless and dangerous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, I had an idea like this one, but I think you did it better than I could have.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I rather like it. There are a few little hang-ups in grammar (omitted commas and run-ons, mostly); I'll fix them if you like. Overall, though, the tone is darn good — especially for a first SCP! — and the effect reasonably interesting. My favorite part is the containment, though; I like the level of detail, and how much the procedures imply about the object itself. They hook you in nicely, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get the sense that this is a prototype object that was meant to be a good thing (hey, surgery with no pain, yay!) but never got finished. Makes me wonder who made it, and why they weren't able to finish. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. The concept is take-it-or-leave-it, but the writing is spot on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The bag only affects things which are intended for cutting or stabbing, which a pencil is not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sorry about the problems that can't be resolved, and I don't really know what to do about the lack-of-a-hook, but I've cut out one of the logs which was rather weak in retrospect, and I'll probably reformat the 'hurt self, didn't notice, died' log. I mostly put it there as an antidote to a potential reader not getting the point (that a lack of a pain response is a serious problem). I might update it to a short-and-sweet 'X personnel have died due to injuries or infections related to SCP-1109'-style addendum later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with Reject in that there's not a hook, but your article still leaves me to wonder how the bag would make those sharp objects things what make people stop feeling. Pretty interesting, if you ask me.

Perhaps the only thing that I'm curious about is that the bag won't affect things what aren't intended to be sharp. That seems subjective; plenty of people would argue that a pencil point should be sharp. I don't know; maybe this is just me.

Great first article, though. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just had a tone-gasm. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find it hard to believe that this is your first article. Very good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm downvoting temporarily for three reasons (not all of which can be fixed, I'm sorry. I'll probably upvote if you fix at least some of them though). First, there does not seem to be a hook. It's a bag what makes things what stop you from hurting. It's just really not scary or thought-provoking, in my mind.

Second, it's a bag what makes things what are anomalous. Those are extremely difficult to do, and I'm not sure this one quite nails it. Third, most of the entries in the log don't add anything to the article except words. They could probably be trimmed down a bit, or at least made more interesting than "he hurt himself and didn't know and then died."

So yeah, small, nitpicky things. Very good for a first SCP though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Im very impressed that this is your first SCP. You did an excellent job. +1!

item.title: This guy gets it.
item.content:encoded:
I like it! Pretty creepy, with some great cringe-inducing details.

Come into the IRC sometime and I can get more specific with what I do and don't like, if you want.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Decided it was probably time to post this after it'd been up in sandbox and forums for a couple of days. This is my first SCP, so please tell me where I (in all likelihood) screwed it up.
